topic: First day at a new job
title: The New Guy
text: I started my new job last Monday. I was so nervous that I arrived thirty minutes early and just sat in the parking lot. When I finally walked in, the receptionist smiled and said, "You must be Andi." She walked me to my desk and introduced me to my team. Everyone seemed nice, but I couldn't remember anyone's name. My manager came over and said, "Don't worry about remembering everything today. Just get a feel for things." By lunchtime, I was wondering if I'd made the right decision leaving my old company. The work looked harder than I expected. But then a colleague invited me to lunch and we talked about everything — the team, the projects, the office coffee machine that nobody knows how to fix. It turns out we went to the same university, just different years. Small world. By the end of the day, I felt a lot better. Not perfect, but better.

comprehension.question: How did Andi feel at the beginning of the day?
What made Andi feel better by the end of the day?
options: The work was easier than expected
He connected with a colleague at lunch
His manager gave him a day off

comprehension.question: What did Andi and his colleague discover about each other?
options: They lived in the same neighborhood
They studied at the same university
They worked at the same company before

vocabulary.meaning_en: person at front desk
person you work with
vocabulary.sentence: a colleague invited me to lunch

title: The Beach Trip
text: My friends and I planned a beach trip for Saturday. We'd been talking about it for weeks. I was about to leave the house when my phone buzzed — a message from Dina: "Sorry guys, something came up. Can't make it." Then Rio said he was feeling sick. Honestly, I felt like cancelling the whole thing. But Sari said, "Come on, let's not put off our plans again. We always do this." She had a point. We always talk about doing things and then end up staying home. So the three of us went. The traffic was terrible — it took two hours instead of one. When we finally got to the beach, it started raining. We just sat in the car, eating snacks and laughing. Sari looked at me and said, "This is actually more fun than the beach." To be honest, she was right. Sometimes the best trips are the ones that go completely wrong.

comprehension.question: Why did the group almost cancel the trip?
options: Two friends couldn't come and the weather was bad
They forgot to book a hotel

comprehension.question: What does Sari mean when she says they 'always do this'?
options: They always go to the beach
They always cancel plans at the last minute
They always invite too many people

comprehension.question: Why couldn't Dina join the trip?

vocabulary.meaning_en: vibrated (phone notification)
deciding not to do something planned
vocabulary.sentence: I felt like cancelling the whole thing

topic: Ordering food when the menu is confusing
title: Lost in the Menu
text: I went to a new restaurant with my girlfriend last night. The menu was in English and Japanese, and I couldn't figure out half of it. There were things like "deconstructed miso" and "umami foam." I had no idea what I was ordering. I was wondering if I should just point at something random. The waiter came over and I panicked. "Could you tell me more about this one?" I asked, pointing at something that cost 180,000 rupiah. The waiter explained it was basically grilled chicken with fancy sauce. I almost laughed — 180k for grilled chicken. My girlfriend ordered the pasta because, as she said, "You can't go wrong with pasta." Fair enough. The food actually came and — not gonna lie — it was delicious. The fancy sauce was worth it. I guess sometimes you just have to trust the menu, even when it doesn't make sense.

target_phrases.phrase: Could you tell me more about

comprehension.question: Why was the narrator confused at the restaurant?
options: The menu had fancy names he didn't understand

comprehension.question: How did the narrator feel about the price?
options: He thought it was too expensive for what it was

comprehension.question: Why did the narrator accept his girlfriend's choice of pasta?
options: She always orders for both of them
He agreed her logic was reasonable

vocabulary.meaning_en: suddenly felt very anxious
vocabulary.sentence: The waiter came over and I panicked

vocabulary.meaning_en: very tasty

topic: Explaining a tech problem to a friend
title: The WiFi Problem
text: My friend called me last night freaking out. "My laptop won't connect to the internet!" she said. "I've tried everything." I asked her what she'd tried so far. "I restarted it. Twice." That was it. That was 'everything.' I told her, "The thing is, restarting doesn't always fix WiFi issues." I walked her through a few steps — checking the WiFi settings, forgetting the network and reconnecting, and restarting the router. Nothing worked. I was running out of ideas. Then I asked, "Is the WiFi working on your phone?" Long pause. "Oh. No, it's not." So the problem wasn't her laptop at all — it was the router. I told her to unplug the router, wait thirty seconds, and plug it back in. Five minutes later she texted: "IT WORKS. You're a genius." I'm not a genius. I just know how to Google things. But I'll take the compliment.

comprehension.question: What was the real cause of the internet problem?
How does the narrator feel about being called a genius?
options: Amused — he knows it was basic troubleshooting

comprehension.question: What did the narrator explain before giving troubleshooting steps?
options: That restarting alone doesn't always solve WiFi issues
That she needed a new laptop
That he didn't know how to help

vocabulary.meaning_en: to disconnect from power
a nice thing someone says about you

title: The Wrong Meeting
text: I showed up to the meeting room at 2 PM sharp. Nobody was there. I checked my calendar — it said "Team sync, 2 PM, Room 3B." I waited ten minutes. Still nobody. I was starting to think I'd gotten the wrong day when my phone buzzed. A message from my team lead: "Where are you? We're all in 4A." I rushed upstairs. Everyone was already mid-discussion. "Sorry for the late reply — I mean, sorry I'm late," I said, mixing up my email language with real life. Everyone laughed. I think there's been a miscommunication about the room number. Someone booked 3B originally, but it got changed yesterday. Just a heads up for next time — always check Slack before heading to any meeting. Apparently the update was posted there. I didn't see it because, honestly, I have 47 unread channels. No one judges me for that, right?

target_phrases.phrase: I think there's been a miscommunication

comprehension.question: Why was the narrator alone in Room 3B?
options: The room had been changed but he didn't know
He came on the wrong day
His team was in a different building

comprehension.question: Why did everyone laugh when the narrator arrived?
options: He accidentally used email language in real life
He tripped on the way in

comprehension.question: What does the narrator suggest people do before going to meetings?

vocabulary.meaning_en: exactly (when talking about time)
based on what you heard or learned
vocabulary.sentence: Apparently the update was posted there

title: The 5 AM Experiment
text: Everyone on the internet says waking up at 5 AM will change your life. So I tried it. Day one: alarm goes off, I hit snooze three times, finally get up at 5:47. Close enough. I made coffee and sat at my desk. It was quiet. Really quiet. I actually got more done in that one hour than I usually do in a whole morning at the office. By the way, the secret isn't waking up early — it's that nobody is messaging you on Slack at 5 AM. Day two: same thing, but this time I couldn't help but fall asleep on the couch at 9 PM. My girlfriend found me passed out with my laptop still open. Day three: I slept through the alarm completely. Looking forward to trying again next week, maybe. To be honest, I think the real lesson is that I'm not a morning person, and that's okay. Some people peak at 5 AM. I peak at 10 PM. It is what it is.

comprehension.question: What was the narrator's overall experience with waking up at 5 AM?
options: It worked briefly but wasn't sustainable for him

comprehension.question: According to the narrator, why was the early morning productive?
options: Nobody was distracting him with messages

comprehension.question: What happened on Day 2 that the narrator couldn't control?
options: He fell asleep very early in the evening
He forgot to set his alarm
He got sick from the coffee

vocabulary.meaning_en: to delay an alarm for a few more minutes
to be at your best performance

topic: Shopping online and getting the wrong item
title: Not What I Ordered
text: I ordered a standing desk from an online store. The photos looked great — clean white surface, adjustable height, modern design. It was supposed to arrive in five days. I was so excited. When the box finally came, I opened it and just stared. It was a desk, technically. But it was tiny. Like, half the size I expected. I looked at the product page again. There it was, in small text I'd completely missed: "Dimensions: 60cm x 40cm." That's not a desk. That's a tray. I should have read the description more carefully. My roommate walked in and said, "Nice laptop stand." Very funny. I wanted to return it, but the shipping cost was almost the same as the desk itself. In the end, I kept it. It's now my coffee table. I'll make it up to myself by ordering a real desk next month — after I triple-check the measurements.

comprehension.question: What was wrong with the desk?
options: It was much smaller than expected

comprehension.question: Why didn't the narrator return the desk?
options: The return shipping was too expensive

comprehension.question: What does the narrator regret not doing?

vocabulary.meaning_en: can be changed to different positions/sizes
the size of something in numbers

title: The Ramen Place
text: There's a new ramen place near my office. Everyone keeps talking about it. I've been meaning to try it for weeks, but the line is always insane — like 30 people waiting outside. Today I finally went. I showed up at 11 AM, thinking I'd beat the crowd. There were already twelve people ahead of me. I waited 40 minutes. The guy behind me in line started chatting. He'd been there four times already. "Is it really that good?" I asked. "Not gonna lie," he said, "it's the best ramen I've had in Jakarta." Bold claim. When I finally sat down and took my first bite, I understood. The broth was rich, the noodles were perfect, and the egg was soft in the middle. I can relate to the four-times-guy now. I'll probably be back next week. The line is annoying, but some things are worth waiting for.

comprehension.question: Why hadn't the narrator tried the restaurant before?
options: The waiting line was always too long
It was too far from his office

comprehension.question: How did the narrator feel after eating the ramen?
options: Disappointed — it wasn't worth the wait
Impressed — he understood why people kept coming back

comprehension.question: What did the narrator intend to do for weeks but didn't?
options: Talk to the guy in line

vocabulary.meaning_en: the liquid base of a soup
crazy, extreme (informal)

title: The Deployment That Wouldn't Die
text: I spent three hours trying to fix a deployment issue yesterday. The build kept failing with an error I'd never seen before. I Googled it. Stack Overflow had a thread from 2019 — not helpful. I tried five different things. Nothing worked. I was swamped with other tasks too, so the pressure was building. Finally, I did what I should have done from the start: I asked for help. I sent a message to the senior engineer: "Hey, do you have a minute? I've been stuck on this for hours." She came over, looked at my screen for thirty seconds, and said, "You're missing a semicolon in the config file." Three hours. One semicolon. I appreciated it, but also wanted to disappear. She laughed and said, "It happens to everyone. I once spent a whole day on a typo." That made me feel better. Lesson learned — ask for help sooner.

comprehension.question: What caused the deployment to fail?
options: A missing semicolon in the config

comprehension.question: Why did the narrator wait so long before asking for help?
options: He didn't think anyone could help
He tried to solve it himself first

comprehension.question: What was the narrator's main takeaway from this experience?
options: You should ask for help earlier instead of struggling alone

vocabulary.meaning_en: releasing code to a server
to go away, become invisible

title: The Long Way Home
text: Jakarta traffic is bad. Everyone knows this. But yesterday was something else. I left the office at 5 PM thinking I'd be home by 7. Normal. Then it started raining. Not regular rain — the kind where you can't see the car in front of you. Google Maps said 3 hours to get home. Three hours. I was stuck on the highway with no exit in sight. I called my wife: "I'm running behind, like, really behind." She said, "No rush, I'll save you dinner." I spent the next two hours listening to a podcast about productivity. Ironic — learning about being productive while sitting in traffic doing absolutely nothing. By the way, I discovered a great podcast though, so it's not all bad. I finally got home at 9:30. My wife had already eaten. The cat was asleep on my chair. Nobody missed me. But the leftover nasi goreng was still warm. That works for me.

comprehension.question: What made the traffic worse than usual?
How does the narrator feel about the overall experience?
options: Accepting — he found a silver lining
He wants to quit his job

comprehension.question: How did the narrator's wife respond to him being late?
options: She told him to take his time and kept food for him

vocabulary.meaning_en: funny because it's the opposite of what you'd expect
vocabulary.sentence: Ironic — learning about being productive while sitting in traffic

vocabulary.meaning_en: food remaining from a previous meal
vocabulary.sentence: the leftover nasi goreng was still warm
